elements.sent-2411-001: Let $p$ be an odd prime and $k$ be an algebraically closed field with characteristic $p$.
elements.sent-2411-002: Booher and Cais showed that the $a$-number of a $\Zp$-Galois cover of curves $\phi: Y \to X$ must be greater than a lower bound determined by the ramification of $\phi$.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this paper, we provide evidence that the lower bound is optimal by finding examples of Artin-Schreier curves that have $a$-number equal to its lower bound for all $p$.
elements.sent-2411-004: Furthermore we use formal patching to generate infinite families of Artin-Schreier curves with $a$-number equal to the lower bound in any characteristic.
elements.sent-2411-005: Let $\phi: Y \to X$ be a smooth, projective, and connected cover of curves over $k$ with Galois group $G$.
elements.sent-2411-007: A classic version of this question concerns the genus of the curves, a standard numerical invariant associated to a curve.
elements.sent-2411-008: The genera of $X$ and $Y$ can be described as the $k$-dimension of $H^0(X,\Omega_X^1)$ and $H^0(Y,\Omega_Y^1)$, the space of regular $1$-forms on $X$ and $Y$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-009: The well known Riemann-Hurwitz formula explains how the genus of $Y$ can be determined entirely from $X$ and ramification information about the cover $\phi$, \begin{equation}2g_Y-2=|G|(2g_X-2)+\sum_{y \in \phi^{-1}(S)}\sum_{i \geq 0}(|G_i(y)|-1),\end{equation} where $S$ is the branch locus of $\phi$ and $G_i(y)$ is the $i$th ramification group in lower numbering at $y$.

elements.sent-2411-011: We will work with the Cartier operator (which is dual to the Frobenius on $H^1(X,\mathcal O_X)$ via Serre duality).

elements.sent-2411-015: Like the genus, the $p$-rank is another invariant of $Y$ that can often be determined from $X$ and ramification information from $\pi$.
elements.sent-2411-016: The Deuring-Shafarevich formula says that when $G$ is a $p$-group, \begin{equation}s_Y-1=|G|(s_X-1)+\sum_{y\in \phi^{-1}(S)}(d_y-1),\end{equation} where $S$ is the branch locus of $\phi$ and $d_y$ is the unique break in the ramification filtration at $y$.
elements.sent-2411-017: We refer to $d_y$ as the ramification break at $y$.
elements.sent-2411-018: The $a$-number is an additional numerical invariant describing the structure of $H^0(X,\Omega_X^1)$, defined as the number of summands in $\bigoplus_i k[\mathcal C_X]/\mathcal C_X^{n_i}$, the first half of \eqref{kCX decomp , i.e. the $k$-dimension of $\ker(\mathcal C_X)$.}

elements.sent-2411-051: An Artin-Schreier curve is a finite morphism of smooth, projective, connected curves $X \to \mathbb P^1$ with Galois group $\Zp$.
elements.sent-2411-052: Any Artin-Schreier curve can be defined by an equation of the form $y^p-y = f(x)$ where $f(x) \in k(x)$ is nonconstant, and $k(x)$ is the function field of $\mathbb P^1$.

elements.sent-2411-362: Patching is the technique of constructing global covers with certain desired properties by building covers locally and gluing them together.
elements.sent-2411-363: Classically, this was done for varieties over the complex numbers, by building spaces on open sets and gluing them together on the intersections.
elements.sent-2411-364: The main tool behind the classical method of patching was Riemann's Existence Theorem, which showed that this topological construction actually leads to an algebraic variety.
elements.sent-2411-365: Formal patching is a technique that generalizes this approach to work over fields other than the complex numbers using formal geometry.
elements.sent-2411-366: In the case of formal patching, covers are built over formal thickenings and glued on their formal overlap.
elements.sent-2411-367: Grothendieck's Existence Theorem algebraizes this construction.
elements.sent-2411-368: For a brief overview on constructing covers using formal patching see \cite{priesoverview , and for a more complete introduction to formal patching and its relationship to complex patching see \cite{harbatergalois .}}
elements.sent-2411-369: In this paper, we use formal patching to glue together curves with known ramification breaks and $a$-numbers to form a curve with a larger ramification break and known $a$-number.
